title: Why Do Conventions Form Without Anyone Deciding?

situation.content:
Your company uses a particular project management tool. It's not the best. Everyone knows better options exist. Nobody switches. When you suggest a change, people nod politely and then keep using the old tool.

Or: your industry has an unwritten norm — maybe it's how proposals are formatted, how pricing is structured, or how meetings are run. The norm isn't optimal. Everyone knows it. It persists anyway.

Or: you're in a country that drives on the left. You visit a country that drives on the right. The choice is completely arbitrary — neither side is "better." Yet each country's convention is as rigid as a law of physics. Switch unilaterally and you die.

These are coordination equilibria: stable patterns that nobody chose, nobody loves, and nobody can unilaterally change — because the only thing that matters is that everyone does the same thing.

story.content:
In 1967, Sweden changed from driving on the left to driving on the right. The date was September 3rd — forever known as "Dagen H" (H-Day, for Högertrafik, right-hand traffic).

At 4:50 AM, all traffic in the country stopped. Every vehicle on every road pulled over. For ten minutes, nothing moved. Then, at 5:00 AM, everyone switched sides. An entire nation changed a fundamental coordination pattern simultaneously.

Why did Sweden need a national event — an act of Parliament, years of planning, special intersections, new road signs, a propaganda campaign — to accomplish something that seems simple? Because driving conventions are Nash equilibria: stable states where no individual can benefit by changing their behavior alone.

John Nash formalized this in 1950: a Nash equilibrium is any situation where every player's strategy is the best response to everyone else's strategy. If everyone drives on the right, your best response is to drive on the right. If everyone drives on the left, your best response is to drive on the left. Both are equilibria. Neither is objectively better. But once you're in one, switching requires everyone to switch simultaneously — which is why it took an act of Parliament.

Sweden's case is instructive because it shows that being trapped in a particular equilibrium isn't about stubbornness or irrationality. Before Dagen H, driving on the left was Sweden's best option — because that's what everyone else was doing, and Sweden's cars had left-hand drive anyway (making right-side driving safer for passing). The country was in the "wrong" equilibrium for its car fleet, everyone knew it, and they'd known it for years. The switch required not individual agreement (most Swedes supported it in theory) but a coordination mechanism powerful enough to move everyone simultaneously.

This is the fundamental challenge of all coordination equilibria: individual rationality keeps you trapped. Each person's best move is to do what everyone else does. So even if a better equilibrium exists, nobody can get there by acting alone. You need either a powerful coordinator (the Swedish government) or a Schelling point so prominent that everyone jumps simultaneously (mission 115).

Nash equilibria explain why entire industries get stuck in suboptimal practices. Every accounting firm uses the same audit methodology — not because it's best, but because clients expect it, regulators accept it, and deviation risks being the only one doing something different. Every social media platform copies every other platform's features — not because they're all optimal, but because users expect them. The equilibrium isn't chosen. It's inherited. And it persists not because it's good, but because deviating alone is worse.
story.title: The Story

contrast.content:
The QWERTY keyboard layout was designed in 1873 by Christopher Latham Sholes for the Remington No. 1 typewriter. Its purpose: prevent mechanical keys from jamming by separating commonly paired letters. The most frequent letter combinations were placed at maximum distance from each other — deliberately slowing typists to prevent physical collisions between the metal arms.

The problem that QWERTY solved hasn't existed for over fifty years. First electric typewriters, then computer keyboards, have zero mechanical jam risk. The constraint that justified QWERTY vanished entirely — but QWERTY remained.

In 1936, August Dvorak patented a layout specifically optimized for typing speed. Studies showed 20-40% faster typing with less finger travel. The US Navy conducted a study during WWII showing that Dvorak-trained typists reached proficiency faster and typed faster at peak performance. Multiple ergonomics studies have confirmed reduced finger strain.

And yet, over 95% of the world types on QWERTY.

This is the most famous example of path dependence — a concept closely related to Nash equilibrium. QWERTY isn't the equilibrium because it's optimal. It's the equilibrium because:

1. **Everyone learned it.** Your personal switching cost is months of retraining.
2. **Every keyboard manufactures it.** A lone Dvorak user can't find keyboards easily.
3. **Every school teaches it.** The pipeline of new typists reinforces the standard.
4. **Every office expects it.** Using a different layout on shared machines is impractical.

The combined switching cost exceeds the combined benefit — even though Dvorak is measurably better. Each individual's best response to a QWERTY world is to use QWERTY. So the suboptimal equilibrium self-perpetuates.

This is both depressing and liberating. Depressing because it means we're surrounded by suboptimal conventions that persist purely because of coordination lock-in. Liberating because it means that existing conventions don't represent "the best way" — they represent "the way everyone already does it." Knowing this, you can stop assuming that convention = optimization and start asking: "Is this actually good, or just locked in?"
contrast.title: The Contrast

principle.content:
A Nash equilibrium is a stable state where everyone's behavior is the best response to everyone else's behavior. Nobody can benefit by unilaterally changing — even if a better equilibrium exists.

Coordination equilibria (driving side, keyboard layout, industry standards) persist not because they're optimal but because deviating alone is costly. They form through historical accident, early adoption, or focal point convergence — and then lock in through network effects and switching costs.

The implication: don't confuse "established" with "optimal." Most conventions in your industry, your team, and your life are Nash equilibria inherited from historical circumstances — not the result of anyone choosing the best option. The question is always: is this convention locked in for structural reasons (switching costs exceed benefits), or is it merely assumed to be locked in because nobody's tried to coordinate a shift?

psychology.content:
Humans have a deep cognitive bias toward assuming that existing conventions are justified — what psychologists call the "status quo bias" and the "system justification" tendency. When we see an established pattern (everyone drives on the right, everyone uses QWERTY, every meeting starts with a status update), we unconsciously assume it persists because it's good, not because switching is costly.

Research by Samuelson and Zeckhauser (1988) demonstrated that people overwhelmingly prefer existing arrangements even when alternatives are objectively superior — simply because they're existing. The mere fact that something is the current state gives it psychological weight beyond its actual merits.

This interacts with coordination equilibria in a dangerous way: not only is switching structurally difficult, we don't even *want* to switch because we've psychologically rationalized the existing convention as good. The QWERTY user doesn't just face switching costs — they've convinced themselves that QWERTY is fine, maybe even optimal ("well, I'm fast on it, so it must work").

The anchoring effect compounds this: once a convention is established, all future alternatives are judged relative to it. A new project management tool isn't judged on absolute merit — it's judged on how much better it would need to be to justify the switching cost from the current one. This asymmetry (incumbents need only be "okay"; challengers need to be dramatically better) is why most innovations fail even when they're genuinely superior.

The most dangerous Nash equilibria are the ones you don't notice — the conventions so entrenched that they feel like laws of nature rather than arbitrary historical choices that got locked in.
psychology.title: Why It Happens

move.content:
1. Distinguish 'optimal' from 'locked in.' For every convention you follow — in your team, your company, your career — ask: is this actually the best way, or is it just what everyone does? If you removed all switching costs, would anyone choose this? The answer reveals whether you're in a good equilibrium or a trapped one.

2. Calculate the real switching cost. Many conventions feel more locked-in than they actually are. The perceived switching cost ("everyone would have to change") often exceeds the actual switching cost ("actually, only our team would need to change, and it would take two weeks"). Test assumptions about lock-in before accepting them.

3. To shift an equilibrium, coordinate simultaneous movement. You can't change a convention alone (that's what makes it an equilibrium). You need enough people to jump together. Sweden's Dagen H worked because the government coordinated everyone at once. In your context: get enough stakeholders committed to switching at the same time that the new option becomes the new focal point.

4. Use new-context moments to escape old equilibria. The easiest time to establish a new convention is when the old context changes: new team formation, new project kickoff, new tool adoption, new leadership. In these moments, no equilibrium is established yet — making it cheap to set a better one.

5. Accept some suboptimal equilibria. Not every locked-in convention is worth fighting. QWERTY costs the average person maybe 5 minutes per day. The coordination cost of switching the entire world isn't worth it. Pick your battles: fight equilibria where the gap between current and optimal is large AND the coordination cost of switching is achievable.
